title: UNIQUE
summary: UNIQUE returns a list of the distinct values from a range, removing duplicates automatically and updating live as the source data changes.
jobUseCases: Build a clean list of distinct regions to feed a dropdown or PivotTable
Count how many unique customers placed orders this month
Generate a deduplicated list of product names from a messy transaction log
Find one-off entries that appear exactly once in a dataset for review
Create a master list of departments from raw HR records for reporting

examples.title: Distinct values from a column
examples.explanation: Given a Region column full of repeats, this spills a list of each region just once (North, South, East, West). The output updates automatically when new regions are added to the source.

examples.title: Count distinct customers
examples.explanation: Wrapping UNIQUE in COUNTA returns how many distinct customer names exist in A2:A100. This is the modern, formula-driven way to get a unique count without a PivotTable.

examples.title: Unique combinations across columns
examples.explanation: Passing a two-column range returns each distinct row combination, e.g. unique Region + Salesperson pairs. Rows that share both values are collapsed into a single entry.

examples.title: Values that appear exactly once
examples.explanation: The third argument exactly_once set to TRUE returns only values that occur a single time in the data, filtering out anything that repeats. Useful for spotting one-off or anomalous entries.

commonMistakes: Confusing the second argument (by_col) with the third (exactly_once); by default both are FALSE
Letting the spill range hit existing data, which triggers a #SPILL! error until the cells are cleared
Treating trailing spaces or case differences as identical; 'North' and 'North ' are counted as two distinct values
Expecting a sorted result; UNIQUE keeps original order, so wrap it in SORT for an ordered list
Using UNIQUE in Excel 2019 or earlier, where the function does not exist
practiceTips: Combine with SORT, like =SORT(UNIQUE(C2:C100)), to get a clean, alphabetised distinct list.
Use TRIM and UPPER on the source first if inconsistent spacing or casing is splitting your values.
Feed a UNIQUE spill range (with #) into Data Validation for an auto-updating dropdown.
Pair UNIQUE with COUNTA for a fast distinct count instead of a helper PivotTable.
